1: The census narrative opens with the most theologically contested verse in 2 Samuel: wayyōsep ʾap YHWH laḥărōt bᵉyiśrāʾēl wayyāset ʾet dāwiḏ bāhem lēmōr lēḵ mᵉneh ʾet yiśrāʾēl wᵉʾet yᵉhûḏāh — ‘the anger of YHWH was kindled again against Israel, and he incited David against them, saying, “Go, number Israel and Judah.”’ The parallel account in 1 Chronicles 21:1 substitutes ‘Satan’ for YHWH as the inciting agent: wayyāqom śāṭān ʿal yiśrāʾēl wayyāset ʾet dāwiḏ — ‘Satan stood against Israel and incited David.’ The two accounts describe the same event from the two levels of OT causality that Job 1-2 makes structurally explicit: YHWH is the primary agent whose sovereign purpose operates through secondary agents including the adversary. The census narrative thus has the same double-agency structure as the Joseph story (Genesis 50:20), the hardening of Pharaoh (Exodus 4:21 + 2 Samuel 7:13), and ultimately the crucifixion (Acts 2:23: ‘this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God... you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men’). The two-level causality is not a theological confusion but the OT’s most honest account of how divine sovereignty and creaturely agency operate simultaneously without collapsing into each other.
14: David’s choice between the three punishments: ṣar lî māʾōḏ nipolāh nāʾ bᵉyaḏ YHWH kî rabbîm raḥămāyw ûḇᵉyaḏ ʾāḏām ʾal ʾeppōlāh — ‘I am in great distress. Let me fall into the hand of YHWH, for his mercy (raḥămîm) is great; but let me not fall into the hand of man.’ David’s theological preference for divine discipline over human punishment is grounded in the comparative mercy argument: YHWH’s raḥămîm (mercies, from reḥem, womb — the Hebrew term for the most intimate form of compassionate attachment) is greater than any human alternative. The word raḥămîm is the plural of intensity — mercies upon mercies. This appeal to YHWH’s raḥămîm is the same ground on which Lamentations 3:22 rests (‘it is of YHWH’s ḥasādîm that we are not consumed; his raḥămîm never come to an end’) and on which Paul declares ‘God of all comfort’ (2 Corinthians 1:3 — the Greek oiktirmōn translating raḥămîm). David’s prayer-logic — preferring the punishment of the merciful God over the punishment of merciless men — is the theological ground of the NT’s call to fear God rather than man (Matthew 10:28).
18: The site of David’s altar-purchase converges three great redemptive-historical moments on a single geographic point. Araunah’s threshing floor (gōren ʾărawnāh) on the ridge above Jerusalem is identified in 2 Chronicles 3:1 as har hammôriyyāh — the Mount Moriah where Solomon builds the temple. This is the same Moriah where Abraham bound Isaac for sacrifice (Genesis 22:2: ‘go to the land of Moriah and offer him there as a burnt offering’). The triple convergence — (1) Abraham’s near-sacrifice on Moriah where YHWH provides a substitute; (2) David’s altar-building where the plague is arrested; (3) Solomon’s temple where YHWH’s presence dwells — makes this the most theologically dense sacred site in the OT, and its anti-type is Golgotha, the hill just outside Jerusalem where the ultimate substitute is offered. The trajectory: Moriah (substitutionary provision), Moriah-as-temple (ongoing atonement), Golgotha (final atonement) — three moments on a single theological line, geographically proximate.
24: David’s refusal of Araunah’s free gift: lōʾ wᵉlōʾ ʾeqnaʾ mēʿimmᵉḵā bᵉmāḥîr wᵉlōʾ ʾaʿăleh laYHWH ʾelōhay ʿōlôt ḥinnām — ‘No, I insist on buying it from you for a price; I will not offer burnt offerings to YHWH my God that cost me nothing (ḥinnām).’ The word ḥinnām (free, for nothing, without cost) is used in Job 1:9 (‘does Job fear God for nothing?’) and in Malachi 1:10 (YHWH’s rebuke of those who perform the motions of worship without genuine commitment). David’s statement defines authentic worship: the sacrifice that costs nothing is not sacrifice. The NT amplifies this: Paul’s living sacrifice (Romans 12:1: ‘present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship’) and Jesus’s parables of the treasure and the pearl (Matthew 13:44-46: ‘in his joy he goes and sells all that he has and buys that field’) — authentic worship and authentic discipleship both cost everything. The free gift refused models the opposite of cheap grace: the worshipper who will not bring to God what costs him nothing has understood something that many who perform free worship have not.
2: Vayomer ha-melekh el-Yoav sar-ha-chayil asher-itto: shut na be-khol shivtei Yisra'el mi-Dan ve-ad Be'ersheva u-phiqdu et-ha-am — David's census command to Joab: shut na (please go around, traverse — shut in the Qal means to go about, roam) through all the tribes of Israel from Dan to Beersheba. The formula mi-Dan ve-ad Be'ersheva (from Dan to Beersheba) is the standard delimitation of the full extent of Israelite territory — Dan in the north (Tel Dan, near the Hermon headwaters), Beersheba in the south (the Negev border). The purpose: u-phiqdu et-ha-am (muster/number the people) — the Piel of paqad (to number, muster, attend to) for a military census.
3: Vayomer Yoav el-ha-melekh: ve-yoseph YHWH elohekha el-ha-am ka-hem ve-kha-hem me'ah pe'amim — Joab's objection: may YHWH multiply the people a hundredfold. Ve-einei adoni ha-melekh ro'ot (and the eyes of my lord the king see it) — the wish that David would live to see the multiplication. Ve-adoni ha-melekh lammah chaphetz ba-davar ha-zeh — why does my lord the king desire this thing? Joab's rare public opposition to a royal command signals the unusual nature of the census; the Deuteronomistic narrator frames his objection as the voice of wisdom the king overrides.
4: Va-yechezeq devar ha-melekh el-Yoav ve-al-sarei he-chayil — “the king's word prevailed (chazaq, was strong) against Joab and the commanders of the army.” Va-yetze Yoav ve-sarei he-chayil lifnei ha-melekh li-phqod et-am Yisra'el — Joab and the army commanders went out from the king's presence to muster Israel. The departure formula marks the execution of a reluctant command — Joab obeys but the narrator has already signaled the objection.
5: Va-ya'averu et-ha-Yarden va-yachanu be-Aro'er li-ymin ha-ir asher be-tokh ha-nachal ha-Gad ve-el-Ya'azer — the census route begins by crossing the Jordan and camping at Aroer — the traditional southern boundary of Reuben and Gad east of the Jordan (Joshua 13:9, 16). Li-ymin ha-ir asher be-tokh ha-nachal (to the right of the city that is in the middle of the wadi) specifies Aroer's position in the Arnon canyon. The systematic geographic progression of the census reflects the administrative reality of counting by region.
6: Va-yavo'u ha-Gil'adah ve-el-eretz Tachtim Chodshi — they came to Gilead and to the land of Tahtim-hodshi (a problematic place name — the MT is uncertain; the LXX reads “the land of the Hittites, to Kadesh”). The census route circles through the Transjordanian territories (Gilead, Jazer), then swings north toward Dan and Sidon, west through the fortified cities of the Hivites and Canaanites, and finally south through Judah to Beersheba — a complete geographic circuit of the entire realm.
7: Va-yavo'u el-mivtzar Tzor ve-khol-arei ha-Chivvi ve-ha-Kena'ani — the census reaches the fortress of Tyre (mivtzar Tzor, the fortified city of Tyre) and all the cities of the Hivites and Canaanites. The inclusion of these non-Israelite territories in the census reflects the extent of Davidic administrative reach — the subjugated peoples of Canaan were subject to David's governance. Va-yetze'u el-negev Yehudah Be'er-sheva — they went out to the Negev of Judah, to Beersheba — completing the circuit at the southern boundary point.
8: Va-yashuttu be-khol-ha-aretz va-yavo'u mi-qetz tish'ah chadashim ve-esrim yom Yerushalaim — they traversed the whole land and came back to Jerusalem at the end of nine months and twenty days. The duration of the census circuit — nine months and twenty days — is the longest time-period specified for a single administrative task in the Succession Narrative, underlining the scope of the undertaking.
9: Va-yitten Yoav et-mispar miphqad-ha-am el-ha-melekh — Joab gave the number of the mustering (mispar miphqad-ha-am) to the king. The double census vocabulary — mispar (number, count) and miphqad (mustering, from paqad) — signals a formal administrative report. The total: va-yehi Yisra'el shemoneh me'ot eleph ish-chayil sholelh cherev ve-ish Yehudah chamesh-me'ot eleph ish — Israel 800,000 able-bodied men (men of valor drawing the sword) and Judah 500,000 men. These numbers are likely administrative totals or hyperbolic summaries rather than literal headcounts.
10: Va-yakh lev David oto acharei-khen sapher et-ha-am — “And David's heart struck him (vayakh lev David oto) after he had numbered the people.” The idiom nakhah lev (the heart strikes) denotes conscience-stricken conviction — it appears in 1 Samuel 24:6 (David's heart struck him after cutting Saul's robe) and here. Va-yomer David el-YHWH: chatati me'od asher asiti — “and David said to YHWH: I have sinned greatly in what I have done.” The confession formula chatati me'od (I have sinned greatly) echoes his confession in 2 Samuel 12 (chatati le-YHWH). Ve-attah YHWH ha'aver na et-avon avdekha ki nivalti me'od — “please take away the iniquity of your servant, for I have acted very foolishly (nivalti, Niphal of naval, to be foolish/worthless).”
11: Va-yaqom David ba-boqer u-devar YHWH hayah el-Gad ha-navi chozeh David lemor — YHWH's word came to Gad the prophet, “seer of David” (chozeh David). The title chozeh (seer, from chazah, to see/envision) is the designation of a prophet who receives visions — distinct from ro'eh (general seer) and navi (spokesman). Chozeh David marks Gad as David's personal court prophet — cf. 2 Chronicles 29:25, where Gad appears as the king's seer who established liturgical arrangements.
12: Halokh ve-dibbart el-David: koh amar YHWH shalosha anokhi notel aleikha — YHWH's offer of three options: shalosha anokhi notel aleikha (three things I am lifting/offering to you). The verb natal (to lift, offer, present) with the Qal participle creates a present-tense offer — the three punishments are being held out as options. Bchar-lekha achat meyhem ve-e'eseh-lakh — choose one of them for yourself and I will do it to you.
13: Va-yavo Gad el-David va-yagged lo va-yomer lo: hata'voa-lekha sheva shanim raav be-artzekha — Gad's presentation of the three options: (1) seven years of famine (raav); (2) three months of flight from your enemies (nos lifnei tzarrekha); (3) three days of plague (dever) in the land. The LXX reads three years for the famine (matching 1 Chronicles 21:12); the MT's seven years aligns with the seven-year famine pattern elsewhere (Gen 41; 2 Kings 8:1). The three options span the three categories of covenant curse: famine, sword (military defeat), and pestilence — the classic triad of Deuteronomic judgment (Deut 28; Lev 26; Jeremiah 14:12; Ezekiel 14:21).
15: Va-yitten YHWH dever be-Yisra'el min-ha-boqer ve-ad et-mo'ed — YHWH sent plague (dever) in Israel from morning until the appointed time. Va-yamot min-ha-am mi-Dan ve-ad Be'ersheva shivim eleph ish — and seventy thousand men of the people died from Dan to Beersheba. The geographic formula mi-Dan ve-ad Be'ersheva (from Dan to Beersheba) is the same as the census formula of 2 Samuel 24:2 — the punishment covers the same extent as the sin. The 70,000 dead creates an appalling reversal: the census that counted the people to strengthen royal power results in the reduction of the people.
16: Va-yishlach et-yado ha-malakh Yerushalaim le-shacheta — “And the angel stretched out his hand toward Jerusalem to destroy it (le-shacheta, Hiphil infinitive of shachat).” Va-yinnachem YHWH el-ha-ra'ah — “and YHWH relented (yinnachem, Niphal of nacham) concerning the evil (ha-ra'ah).” The verb nacham (to relent, be comforted, change course) in YHWH's response is the same verb used for YHWH relenting from destruction elsewhere (Exodus 32:14, Jonah 3:10, Amos 7:3). Ha-malakh asher-hishchit ba-am (the angel who was destroying among the people) — beside the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.
17: Vayomer David el-YHWH be-re'otho et-ha-malakh ha-makkeh ba-am — David's intercessory prayer when he saw the angel striking the people. Vayomer: hinneh anokhi chatati ve-anokhi he'eveti — “Behold, I sinned and I did wickedly (he'eveti, Hiphil of avah, to act perversely/crookedly).” Ve-elleh ha-tson mah asu — “and these, the sheep, what have they done?” The shepherd-and-flock metaphor — the king as shepherd whose guilt falls on the innocent flock — is the most direct statement of vicarious suffering in the Succession Narrative. David's identification of himself as the guilty party and the people as innocent sheep applies the covenant-kingship logic: the shepherd bears responsibility for the flock's condition.
19: Va-ya'al David ki-devar Gad asher tzivvah YHWH — David went up according to the word of Gad, which YHWH commanded. The obedience formula — ki-devar Gad asher tzivvah YHWH (according to the word of Gad which YHWH commanded) — frames David's action as prophetically directed compliance rather than royal initiative. The sanctuary-founding narrative that follows (2 Samuel 24:18–25) is the theological climax of 2 Samuel: the site of David's altar is the future site of Solomon's Temple.
20: Va-yashqeph Araunah va-yar et-ha-melekh ve-avadav ovirim alav — Araunah looked out (shaqaph, to look down from a height) and saw the king and his servants crossing toward him. Va-yetze Araunah va-yishtachu la-melekh appaiv artzah — Araunah went out and prostrated himself before the king, faces to the ground. The prostration of the Jebusite threshing-floor owner before the king is both courtly convention and a recognition of David's royal authority over the city — Araunah's native city, which David conquered (2 Samuel 5:6–9).
21: Vayomer Araunah: maddua va adoni ha-melekh el-avdo — “why has my lord the king come to his servant?” Va-yomer David: liqnot me-imkha et-ha-goren le-vnot mizbeach la-YHWH — “to buy (qanah) from you the threshing floor, to build an altar to YHWH.” The verb qanah (buy, acquire — also to create/possess, as in Genesis 14:19, YHWH as qoneh shamayim va-aretz) is the foundational act: the Temple site begins with a legitimate purchase, not royal confiscation. The contrast with Ahab's seizure of Naboth's vineyard (1 Kgs 21) is implicit: David buys what kings in the ancient Near East routinely took.
22: Vayomer Araunah el-David: yiqach va-ya'al adoni ha-melekh ha-tov be-einav — “let my lord the king take and offer up what seems good to him.” Araunah offers everything: the oxen for burnt offerings (olot), the threshing sledges and the yokes of the oxen for wood. The generosity of the Jebusite's offer is genuine — he provides both the sacrifice animals and the wood from his agricultural equipment, giving up his livelihood for the king's worship.
23: Ha-kol natan Araunah ha-melekh la-melekh — “all this Araunah the king gives to the king.” The unusual double use of ha-melekh (the king) — Araunah ha-melekh (Araunah the king) and la-melekh (to the king) — is textually difficult. Ha-melekh applied to Araunah may be an honorific title (“Araunah the noble/lord”) or a textual variant. The Septuagint reads “Ornan gave all these things to the king” without the title. Va-yomer Araunah el-ha-melekh: YHWH elohekha yirtzekha — and Araunah said to the king: may YHWH your God accept you (yirtzekha, Qal jussive of ratzah, to accept favorably, be pleased with).
25: Va-yiven sham David mizbeach la-YHWH va-ya'al olot u-shelamim — “And David built there an altar to YHWH and offered burnt offerings (olot) and peace offerings (shelamim).” The olah (whole burnt offering) represents total consecration to YHWH; the shelamim (peace offering, from shalom) represents the restored covenant relationship between God and people. The double offering — total devotion and covenant peace — is the theological summary of the chapter and of 2 Samuel: David the sinful king seeks and receives restoration through sacrifice. Va-ye'ater YHWH la-aretz va-te'atzar ha-magepha me-al Yisra'el — “and YHWH responded to the entreaty for the land and the plague was stopped from Israel.” The verb ya'ater (Niphal of atar, to be entreated, to respond to entreaty) — the same verb used for the resolution of the Gibeonite blood-guilt in 2 Samuel 21:14 — ties the two appendix narratives together: both crises required covenant atonement; both were resolved by divine response to human sacrifice. The threshing floor of Araunah becomes the site of Solomon's Temple (2 Chronicles 3:1) — the final altar of David's reign prefigures the permanent dwelling place of YHWH's Name.
